title: Safety Inspection Patterns
body: Every major NCR at ADW traces back to the same root cause: supervision quality, not equipment failure. The ROM pad wrong-tipping NCR (25-054) and the ZR-24 exclusion breach (26-012) both happened with a supervisor nearby who didn't catch it — the pattern is repeating, not a one-off.
keyPoints: The ROM pad is the highest-stakes zone on site — it shows up in the SIF report, two major NCRs, and every monthly inspection. Unlabeled stockpile fingers next to high-grade material is a wrong-tipping risk that's already cost the site once.

Refuelling areas keep generating incidents — four now, across different parties (gas station near-miss, MG-85 wall strike, North Go-Line hoses on the floor, NCR 25-037). The common gap every time: no spotter or assistant present during fuelling.
Dust is a control-gap amplifier, not just a nuisance. It hides delineators, cuts visibility approaching the crusher, and was the stated cause in the ZR-24 breach.
Findings cluster at shift-end, shift-start, and pre-dawn — the supervision-thinnest windows. Treat handovers as higher-risk, not routine.
Recurring findings (signage, LV parking, delineators) should get written into the standing inspection checklist instead of being rediscovered every pass.
apply: If you're near the ROM pad or a refuelling bay, check who's actually supervising — that single missing control has caused more incidents at ADW than any equipment fault or procedure gap.
